articles.headline: Letter from William Yates, Esq.: Slavery and Colored People in Delaware (concluded)
articles.author: William Yates, Esq.
articles.summary: Conclusion of Yates's investigative report on Delaware, examining the state's manumission statutes in detail (an 1810 law holding slaves promised future freedom as slaves 'in the mean time'; an 1767 law calling free Black people 'idle and slothful'), interviewing multiple white Delawareans about slavery's economic drag on the state, and profiling several free Black tradesmen of substance in Camden and Dover -- a brick mason with $1,000 credit, a hotel builder, and others -- as evidence Black residents could prosper if allowed. Includes a reprinted account from the Herald of Freedom on 'Ministerial Neutrality,' criticizing clergy who refuse to take an anti-slavery stand, and a remarkable eyewitness account (signed W. J. Wilson, a New York bootmaker) of a formerly enslaved Baptist minister, Frank Spence, in Nassau, New Providence (Bahamas), who built his own church with his own hands and earnings after purchasing freedom for himself and his family.

articles.headline: Broadway Tabernacle -- editorial on segregated pews and Black attendance
articles.summary: The editor objects to seeing Black worshippers crowded into the southern tier of pews at Broadway Tabernacle, arguing Black New Yorkers should instead worship at their own churches or scatter thinly among many white congregations rather than concentrate visibly in one place and thereby reinforce the practice of separate/proscribed seating; frames the practice as spiritual oppression comparable to that of an earthly despot and urges gradual, dignified pressure for integration.

articles.headline: Mr. Pattinson's Temperance Eating House -- Samuel Cornish denied tea on account of color; Thomas Van Rensselaer cleared of malicious-motive accusation
articles.author: editorial, with letter from Thomas Van Rensalaer
articles.summary: Follow-up coverage of the refusal of a cup of tea to a Black clergyman (the editor, Rev. S. E. Cornish) at H. Pattinson's Temperance Eating House, Cedar and Nassau Sts., New York, on account of color. The editor rebuts the charge that Thomas Van Rensalaer publicized the incident to injure Pattinson's business, printing Van Rensalaer's own letter (dated New York, August 20, 1837) denying he authored or motivated the original report and affirming he never discussed his principles with Pattinson beforehand. Additional correspondence weighs whether prejudice against color among Black people themselves (over shades of complexion) is a separate, under-discussed evil, and reprints a lengthy petition-organizing letter signed 'A Colored American' urging Rev. Cornish to keep exposing such incidents.

articles.headline: Moral Reform Convention -- Editor's Correspondence from Philadelphia
articles.author: editor (S. E. Cornish), datelined Philadelphia
articles.summary: The editor, visiting Philadelphia -- birthplace of Absalom Jones, Richard Allen, and other 'noble dead' -- reports on the opening of the American Moral Reform Convention, presided over by the venerable James Forten Sr. (President) and Jacob C. White (Vice President), with Robert Purvis, James Forten Jr., and William Whipper as Secretaries. Frames Pennsylvania's Black community as uniquely positioned near the South to demonstrate the injustice of slavery and the feasibility of emancipation.

articles.headline: A Call to Colored Young Men -- Philip A. Bell and Charles B. Ray to tour the Hudson River counties for suffrage organizing
articles.summary: Announces that Philip A. Bell (proprietor) and Rev. Charles B. Ray (General Agent) will tour the towns and villages of the Hudson River border to organize colored young men on the urgent question of the 1821 disenfranchisement, calling a public meeting at the African Methodist Episcopal Church, New York, and listing an extensive roster of committee signers including Wm. A. Tyson, Chs. S. Sidney (Thomas S. Sidney), Samuel Miles, Wm. B. Solucon, Edward V. Clark, John J. Zuille, Francis Myers, Daniel J. Elston, Edward Gills, Timothy Seaman, Albro Lyons, Ulysses C. Vidal, Peter Guignon, Wm. J. Wilson, P. Vogelsang Jr., George Kissam, H. Highland Garnet, Thomas Harris, James P. Harris, Jeremiah Mitchell, and Chas. L. Reason, dated New York, August 1, 1837.

articles.headline: To the Colored Citizens of the State of New York -- Public Meeting resolution on the disfranchising property clause
articles.summary: Minutes of a Public Meeting of colored inhabitants of the City and County of New York, held August 9, 1837, resolving that the $250 unincumbered-real-estate property requirement imposed on Black (but not white) voters by the revised State Constitution is degrading and unjust, and pledging immediate measures to have the clause stricken.

articles.headline: "Facts" series on free Black Philadelphia's institutions, wealth, and taxation
articles.author: signed 'C.'
articles.summary: A numbered series of statistical 'Facts' on free Black Philadelphians: fifteen churches worth $172,400 with 34 ministers, 17 Sunday schools, a public library of ~2,000 volumes plus 438 in private libraries, three debating societies, two Female Literary societies, two tract societies, two Bible societies, two temperance societies; annual house/ground/water rents and newspaper spending; 55 Beneficial Societies with combined mutual-aid funds; assertions that Black Philadelphians pay their own poor-relief costs and additional sums toward white poor relief; and profiles of individual self-made men (a formerly enslaved man who purchased freedom for himself and his wife and built a substantial Philadelphia property; another who bought his freedom twice after being cheated the first time).

articles.headline: Robert Banks, Buffalo agent, forwards $20 and reports a successful Aug. 1 Emancipation Day celebration
articles.author: Robert Banks
articles.summary: Buffalo agent Robert Banks writes (Aug. 2, 1837) that David Ruggles is en route to Buffalo, encloses $20 (including $5 each from Samuel T. Wilcox and Charles Williams for the editor's salary fund), and reports a delightful Aug. 1 British West India Emancipation celebration with a collection taken up for the paper.

articles.headline: Reply to Clarksox: Slavery and the slave trade in the District of Columbia, authorized by Congress
articles.summary: Extended constitutional argument rebutting Judge Cranch's claim that Congress does not license slave traders in Washington DC, tracing Maryland's and Virginia's 1788/1789 cession of the federal district, Congress's exclusive legislative jurisdiction over it, and a series of DC municipal ordinances (1802-1816) taxing and licensing the domestic slave trade, jailing free Black travelers, and whipping enslaved people for a fee -- concluding Congress bears direct moral and legal responsibility for slavery and the slave trade in the nation's capital. Quotes Vice President Richard M. Johnson's own 1820 speech (as a slaveholding Missouri-restriction opponent) conceding Congress's exclusive power to abolish slavery in DC.

articles.headline: Moral Reform Convention -- editorial critique of the Philadelphia Moral Reform Society
articles.author: editor (S. E. Cornish)
articles.summary: Sharply critical follow-up on the American Moral Reform Convention in Philadelphia: praises individual leaders (James Forten Sr. as President; Robert Purvis and William Whipper singled out for character) but faults the Society itself as directionless, adopting 'vague' universal objects instead of concrete plans targeted at Black uplift, and reports internal disorder in the proceedings. Notes James Forten Jr. delivered a much-admired hour-plus address on education, and that John C. Bowers of Philadelphia and 'Mr. Cook of Washington City' both spoke on temperance.

articles.headline: ON DRESS -- letter signed 'Agnes', Bucks County
articles.author: Agnes
articles.summary: A mother in Bucks County, PA writes urging parents to dress children plainly and teach that merit, not clothing, confers respectability, illustrated with a fictionalized mother-and-children dialogue on conchology (shell science) as a model of instructive family conversation; includes a full didactic dialogue about a Nautilus shell specimen.

articles.headline: Anti-Slavery doctrinal essay: 'What are the Doctrines of Anti-Slavery Men' (Part 1)
articles.type: reprint (from the Alton Observer) / doctrinal essay
articles.summary: First installment of a systematic statement of abolitionist first principles: slavery as sin against God and the enslaved; the duty of immediate repentance/emancipation; the master's sole responsibility (not the original importers') for holding people in bondage; extensive quotation of pro-slavery Senator Calhoun's own property-rights language turned against him; and practical guidance that emancipation must be voluntary but abolitionists' role is to persuade, drawing an extended analogy to the temperance movement's persuasion-not-coercion model. Followed by a historical list of all U.S. Presidents, Vice Presidents, and cabinet officers to date, framed as a reference item.

articles.headline: Public Meeting of the Colored Young Men of New York, Philomathean Hall -- suffrage organizing resolutions
articles.summary: Full minutes of a meeting of colored young men of New York City held at Philomathean Hall, August 21, 1837, called to organize systematic annual petitioning for suffrage. Timothy Seaman elected President; Edward V. Clark, Henry Highland Garnet, and Charles L. Reason Vice Presidents; John J. Zuille and George Downing Secretaries. Resolutions moved by Peter P. Simons, Thomas S. Sidney, Henry Highland Garnet, and Daniel Elston invoke the War of 1812 service of Black New Yorkers as grounds for suffrage and establish a Standing Corresponding Committee of ten (Henry H. Garnet, Timothy Seaman, Wm. J. Wilson, Thomas Harris, P. P. Simons, John J. Zuille, T. S. Sidney, Chas. Reason, George Kissam, Peter Guignon) to coordinate petitioning statewide, plus ward-level committees. Also includes the constitution of the Young Men's Literary and Moral Reform Society of Pittsburgh (auxiliary to the American Moral Reform Society), founded Feb. 17, 1837, naming founders Zulacher Newman (President), Wm. J. Granly (Vice President), Orang Lewis (Recording Secretary), Martin R. Delany (Librarian), John N. Templeton (Corresponding Secretary), and counsellors Charles T. Williamson, John H. Butler, U. A. Johnson, Matthew Jones, Wm. Barley, Benjamin Jones, Edward Parker.

articles.headline: Letter from General Agent Charles B. Ray -- steamboat tea-service discrimination on the Hudson
articles.author: Rev. Charles B. Ray
articles.summary: Charles B. Ray, traveling with Philip A. Bell aboard the steamboat James Madison to Newburgh (Aug. 15, 1837), recounts in detail being refused tea in the ship's cabin and told to eat in the kitchen with 'all persons of color'; after firm but polite insistence on 'principle,' the captain's representatives never relent. Ray continues on to Poughkeepsie, Troy (preaching at the Seamen's Bethel Church and a Presbyterian church, meeting Thomas Van Rensselaer and Wm. Yates), and describes visiting Mount Ida above Troy, before proceeding toward Albany and Utica; encloses 30 new subscriber names from Troy.

articles.headline: Letter from Agent John N. Templeton, Pittsburgh
articles.author: John N. Templeton
articles.summary: Templeton, corresponding secretary of the new Pittsburgh Young Men's Literary and Moral Reform Society (16 members), writes to Cornish pledging support for the paper as a unifying force for Black Americans.

articles.headline: Letter from Philadelphia describing the Moral Reform Convention's internal fight over the word 'colored'
articles.author: unsigned, datelined Philadelphia, Aug. 21, 1837
articles.summary: A detailed insider account of the American Moral Reform Convention's committee report recommending the establishment of schools for the 'free people of color,' which was initially adopted but later reversed on a second vote (helped by a contested chair ruling) after objectors argued the designation 'colored' implied degradation and that the Society's work should extend to the white population too -- an internal ideological fight the correspondent calls disgraceful and 'blind and furious.'

articles.headline: Letter to the Editor from F. A. Hinton, Philadelphia, warning against dropping racial identity in reform work
articles.author: F. A. Hinton
articles.summary: Frederick A. Hinton writes that reformers who refuse to identify with 'the colored man' as such, rather than only with 'condition,' will fail, drawing parallels to Moses's identification with his enslaved people and to British abolitionists Chatham, Wilberforce, and Pitt who fought for changed condition, not changed color.

articles.headline: Cincinnati correspondence -- George Cary reports on subscription efforts and a Toronto visit
articles.author: George Cary
articles.summary: Cincinnati agent George Cary (Aug. 13, 1837) reports difficulty raising subscriptions amid tight money, mentions a recent visit to Toronto, Upper Canada, and hands off day-to-day agency work to a Mr. Wm. M. Jolson (successor to Cary as Liberator agent in Cincinnati), enclosing $20.

articles.headline: Troy meeting resolutions on Charles B. Ray's visit -- William Rich and Edward Bishop
articles.author: William Rich, Edward Bishop
articles.summary: Resolutions from a Troy meeting of the colored community (Aug. 21-22, 1837) welcoming Charles B. Ray's visit and Thomas Van Rensselaer's presence, pledging fifty new subscribers to the Colored American; signed by William Rich and Edward Bishop.

articles.headline: Slave Case in Bucks County -- Basil Dorsey (alias Ephraim) discharged; Robert Purvis and David Paul Brown
articles.author: W. J. (correspondent), reprinted from the National Inquirer
articles.summary: Detailed eyewitness account of the Doylestown, PA hearing (fall 1837) of alleged fugitive slave Basil Dorsey, alias Ephraim, claimed by T. Sollers of Maryland. Judge Fox ruled the claimant's proof of Maryland slave law inadmissible (an uncertified volume of statutes was rejected), and the claimant's request for a continuance was refused; Dorsey was ordered discharged amid a large, tense crowd. Robert Purvis is credited as chiefly responsible for the defense's vigilance and promptness (retaining counsel David Paul Brown pro bono); a wagon was waiting and Dorsey was spirited away amid cheers as the crowd escorted him from town.

articles.headline: A Plea for Poor Gospel Ministers
articles.author: 'R.S.'
articles.summary: An appeal for a benevolent society to support poor and aged Black clergy, describing the acute hardships of underpaid Black ministers (families in want, no institutional safety net) compared to white clergy, and calling on named philanthropists ('our Tappans, our Jays, and our Smiths') to help establish such a fund.

articles.headline: An Address on Non-Resistance to Offensive Aggression
articles.author: William Whipper, of Columbia, Pa.
articles.summary: William Whipper's major pacifist address arguing non-resistance to physical aggression is consistent with reason, tracing the argument through classical and biblical exempla (Aristides, the Sermon on the Mount), condemning war generally (citing Thomas Dick's global casualty estimates) and urging Black Americans specifically to renounce violent self-defense/retaliation as inconsistent with true reform, framed as an installment to be continued.

articles.headline: Editorial: 'Increase of Prejudice' -- abolitionist consciousness-raising explained
articles.summary: Argues that apparent 'increased' white prejudice since abolitionism's rise is not really an increase but the visible friction of Black Americans now asserting equality as a right rather than accepting paternalistic favors, comparing the psychological shift to Ottoman Turks' difficulty accepting a 'Christian Dog' as an equal, and citing personal experience of Dr. Spring's congregation as an example of conditional white tolerance predating abolitionism's moral clarity.

articles.headline: William Whipper's public letter defending the Moral Reform Society against editor Cornish's criticism
articles.author: William Whipper
articles.summary: Whipper's point-by-point rebuttal (dated Columbia, July 29, 1837) of Cornish's editorial criticism of the American Moral Reform Society, defending named leaders James Forten Sr., Joseph Cassey (Treasurer), Jacob C. White, John P. Burr, Robert Purvis, Rev. Morris Brown, Rev. Charles W. Gardner, Frederick A. Hinton, Joshua Brown, John B. Roberts, Stephen H. Gloucester, James McCrummill, Thomas Butler, and Daniel A. Payne as men of high character, arguing the Society's broad, universal objects (education, temperance, economy, peace, universal liberty) are deliberately not race-restricted, and that disorder at the Convention reflected participation by outside/allied delegates rather than the Society's own members. Cornish's blistering reply follows in the same issue, restating that the Society is 'UNWORTHY' of confidence until it defines concrete race-specific objects.

articles.headline: Return of Dr. James McCune Smith -- welcomed home from five years' medical study in Scotland
articles.summary: Warmly welcomes Dr. James McCune Smith back to New York after five years studying medicine in Scotland (Glasgow), praising his character and scholarship and calling on the city's physicians to extend him professional courtesy and patronage rather than perpetuate the prejudice that drove him to study abroad in the first place.

articles.headline: David Ruggles's monthly report to the New York Committee of Vigilance
articles.author: David Ruggles
articles.summary: Ruggles's letter (Aug. 1837) to the Chairman of the New York Committee of Vigilance, written from the country due to declining health, reports 19 cases relieved and 5 unresolved for lack of funds during the past month, includes a financial statement (receipts $32, expenses $54, balance against the treasury $22), and clarifies that William Dixon is not in the hands of 'a gang of desperadoes' as rumored but has retired to the country on his friends' advice.

articles.headline: Farewell Dinner to Dr. James M'Cune Smith, Glasgow -- full toast and speech, reprinted from the Glasgow Chronicle
articles.summary: Full account of a farewell dinner given to Dr. James McCune Smith by fellow students and friends at the Tontine Hotel, Glasgow, on his departure for America after completing his M.D. Chairman John Crawford's toast is followed by Smith's own extended, eloquent speech, in which he describes an earlier insult -- refusal of a cabin passage from the Broomielaw to New York by an American captain solely because of his complexion -- and thanks his Glasgow friends for demonstrating that such prejudice is not universal, vowing the honor will strengthen his resolve against American racial prejudice.

events.name: American Moral Reform Convention -- Philadelphia, opening session
events.description: Opening session of the first annual meeting of the American Moral Reform Society's Convention, convened at 10 o'clock; James Forten Sr. presiding as President, Jacob C. White as Vice President, with Robert Purvis, James Forten Jr., and William Whipper as Secretaries.

events.name: Public Meeting of Colored Citizens of New York -- suffrage property-clause resolutions
events.description: Public meeting of colored inhabitants of the City and County of New York resolving against the $250 unincumbered-real-estate voting requirement imposed uniquely on Black citizens under the revised State Constitution, and resolving to seek its repeal.

events.name: Public Meeting of the Colored Young Men of the City of New York -- Philomathean Hall
events.description: Meeting to organize a statewide effort by colored young men to petition the Legislature for suffrage; established a Standing Corresponding Committee of 10 and passed resolutions invoking Black veterans' War of 1812 service.

events.name: Founding of the Young Men's Literary and Moral Reform Society of Pittsburgh
events.description: Founding of a young men's literary and moral reform society in Pittsburgh, auxiliary to the American Moral Reform Society, adopting a constitution and electing officers; initial membership of 16.

events.name: Bucks County fugitive slave hearing -- Basil Dorsey (alias Ephraim) discharged
events.description: Hearing before Judge Fox on the claim of T. Sollers of Maryland to Basil Dorsey, alias Ephraim, as a fugitive slave; the claimant's proof of Maryland slave law was ruled inadmissible and Dorsey was ordered discharged amid a large crowd. Robert Purvis organized the defense (retaining David Paul Brown pro bono) and arranged Dorsey's departure from town.

events.name: Farewell dinner for Dr. James M'Cune Smith -- Tontine Hotel, Glasgow
events.description: Farewell dinner given by fellow students and friends to Dr. James McCune Smith on completing his M.D. at the University of Glasgow and departing for New York; chaired by John Crawford, Esq., with toasts and speeches praising Smith and denouncing American racial prejudice.
